tree.heading: Managing your food and fluid intake when on haemodialysis
tree.content_preview:
If you are on haemodialysis this leaflet will help you manage your food and fluid intake. Your dietitian will also be able to answer any questions or concerns.

When your kidneys are not working prope

tree.heading: Protein
tree.content_preview:
Protein is needed for the growth and repair of all body tissues. This includes maintaining your muscle strength.

A small amount of protein is lost during the dialysis process. Therefore, to ensure th

tree.heading: Salt
tree.content_preview:
We would advise you to reduce salt in your diet. Reducing salt can help:
Reduce feelings of thirst (useful if you are on a fluid restriction).
Prevent carrying extra fluid (oedema).
Reduce blood press

tree.heading: List of high and lower salt alternative foods
DAIRY Higher salt foods
tree.content_preview: Cheese spread (such as Dairylea, Primula), hard cheeses (such as cheddar, edam, stilton, halloumi)

tree.heading: SAVOURY SNACKS Higher salt foods
tree.content_preview: Crisps, salted nuts, salted snacks (such as Twiglets, Pretzels), Ritz biscuits, TUC biscuits, mini cheddars

tree.heading: MISCELLANEOUS Higher salt foods
tree.content_preview: Marmite, Bovril, stock cubes, gravy granules, tinned or packet soups, Pot Noodles, bottled sauces such as soy sauce and tomato ketchup

tree.heading: Lower salt alternatives
tree.content_preview: Cottage cheese, cream cheese (such as Philadelphia), mozzarella

tree.heading: Lower salt alternatives
tree.content_preview: Plain popcorn, plain breadsticks, cream crackers, Ryvita, rice cakes

tree.heading: Lower salt alternatives
tree.content_preview:
Natex savoury spread, Meridian yeast extract, low or zero salt stock cubes, reduced salt gravy granules or homemade gravy, homemade soup, reduced salt sauces, vinegar

If you have been advised to foll

tree.heading: Understanding food labelling
tree.content_preview: Approximately 75% of our salt intake comes from ready-made foods. It is important to look at food labels when making decisions about which foods to buy. Remember, food labels may show the salt content

tree.heading: Fluid
tree.content_preview: When your kidneys are not working properly you are not able to get rid of all the fluid that you drink. Haemodialysis helps to remove fluid from the body, but if you drink more than is being removed y

tree.heading: Your fluid allowance = 500ml + previous days 24-hour urine output
tree.content_preview: Most people on dialysis pass less urine over time; if this happens you will need to have your fluid allowance reassessed and will need to reduce your fluid intake. Ask your haemodialysis nurse or diet

tree.heading: What counts as fluid?
tree.content_preview:
all hot and cold drinks
soup, gravy, thin sauces
jelly, ice lollies
ice cubes.

Fluid rich foods such as custard, yoghurt and ice cream count as half the volume of fluid, for example 120ml yoghurt = 6

tree.heading: Tips to help you keep to your fluid allowance:
tree.content_preview:
Use a measuring jug or water bottle to help manage your allowance.
Use a smaller cup or only fill your cup or mug to halfway.
T ry to drink only when you are thirsty rather than out of habit or to be

tree.heading: Potassium
tree.content_preview: Potassium is a mineral found naturally in foods. Haemodialysis removes potassium from the blood, but levels build up between dialysis sessions. It is therefore often necessary to restrict dietary pota

tree.heading: The target potassium level is 3.5-5.9 mmol/ L.
tree.content_preview: A high level of potassium in your blood can be dangerous for your heart. However, the amount of potassium you can include in your diet is very individual and not everyone on haemodialysis needs to fol

tree.heading: List of foods that are high in potassium
tree.content_preview:
| Potato | Non-boiled potatoes, such as chips, jacket potato, hash browns, potato waffles |
|------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Vegeta

tree.heading: Phosphate
tree.content_preview:
Phosphate is a mineral found naturally in foods and is also used as a food additive.

Phosphate levels in the blood can increase when the kidneys are not working properly.

Over time, this can lead to

tree.heading: The target phosphate level is 1.1-1.7mmol/L.
tree.content_preview: Haemodialysis removes some phosphate from the blood, but it may be necessary to have less phosphate in your diet as well. You may also have been prescribed a medicine called a phosphate binder, which

tree.heading: Fibre
tree.content_preview:
Fibre is important to prevent constipation and keep your bowels regular.

Foods high in fibre that are good for you to eat are:
Wholemeal or granary bread instead of white bread.
Whole wheat breakfast

tree.heading: Eating when on Dialysis
tree.content_preview:
During haemodialysis, it is still important to follow your individual diet. It is a good idea to eat a sandwich or other protein containing snack on dialysis if you can.

If you eat foods that are hig

tree.heading: If you have a poor appetite
tree.content_preview:
Eat little and often, have small meals with nourishing snacks in between.
Cold foods can sometimes be more appetising, try a sandwich or chilled dessert.
If there are times of the day when your appeti

tree.heading: Keeping Active
tree.content_preview: When you first start haemodialysis, you may feel tired and lack energy. However, dialysis should not prevent you from being physically active. In fact, it can help with your overall health. Benefits i

tree.heading: Breakfast
tree.content_preview:
Porridge
Weetabix, Shredded Wheat, Shreddies, Rice Krispies or Cornflakes
Bread or toast with butter/spread and jam/marmalade
Boiled/scrambled or poached egg with bread or toast
English muffin with bu

tree.heading: Light meal
tree.content_preview:
Sandwiches, rolls, pitta bread or bagels
Sandwich fillings - fresh chicken, turkey, beef, lamb, pork, cottage cheese, cream cheese, egg, tuna, salmon (fresh or tinned boneless in oil or spring water),

tree.heading: Main meal
tree.content_preview:
Chicken or mixed bean casserole with mashed potato and broccoli
Spaghetti Bolognese (prepared with 400g tinned tomatoes to serve 4)
Lamb, beef, chicken, lentil, chickpea curry with rice or chapatti
Ro

tree.heading: Desserts
tree.content_preview:
Fruit pie, fruit crumble, tinned fruit (juice drained off), fresh fruit, pavlova
Y oghurt or fromage frais, ice cream, custard, rice pudding, trifle, crème caramel
Soya yogurts.

tree.heading: Snack foods
tree.content_preview:
Cucumber, carrot, pepper sticks with hummus, cream cheese or tzatziki
Breadsticks, cream crackers, water biscuits, oat cakes, crispbread, rice cakes
Bread, toast, English muffin, croissant
Vegetable o

tree.heading: Drinks
tree.content_preview:
Water, tonic water, soda water, flavoured water
Tea, 1x weak instant coffee per day
Squash, cordial
Light coloured fizzy drinks - e.g. lemonade, Fanta, Sprite.

tree.heading: Oxford Kidney Unit
tree.content_preview:
The website has lots of information about the Oxford Kidney Unit for patients and carers.

Website: www.ouh.nhs.uk/oku

tree.heading: Kidney Care UK
tree.content_preview:
A charity which has lots of practical support and information for people with kidney disease.

Website: www.kidneycareuk.org

tree.heading: Six Counties Kidney Patients Association
tree.content_preview:
The SCKPA is run for patients by patients or family members.

They offer support to people suffering from kidney disease or who are on dialysis. They work closely with the Oxford Kidney Unit and have

tree.heading: National Kidney Federation
tree.content_preview:
A charity which has lots of practical support and information for people with kidney disease.

Website:

www.kidney.org.uk

tree.heading: Health for Me (patient portal)
tree.content_preview: Health for Me enables you to access to your digital health record via the OUH Patient Portal. It is an online system that allows you to easily view parts of your digital health record safely and secur

tree.content_preview:
If you would like an interpreter, please speak to the department where you are being seen.
